overview.title: Architecture Deep-Dive & Threading Model
overview.description: FluxUI is a high-performance, GPU-first desktop application shell and styling toolkit built in C++ with native bindings for Rust and Zig.
overview.p1: Historically, desktop UI frameworks have relied either on bulky HTML/CSS browser runtimes (like Electron) or legacy immediate-mode rasterization structures that execute repeated CPU-side painting algorithms every frame. FluxUI departs from this legacy by implementing a **GPUI-like architecture** (similar to the engine powering Zed).
overview.p2: The core philosophy is simple: **application logic defines a retained DOM-like tree of widgets, but the actual compilation of coordinates, styling cascades, and rendering draw lists is deferred to a specialized hardware-accelerated pipeline.**

threading.title: 🔄 1. Threading Model & Frame Pacing
threading.p1: FluxUI separates the user-interface application logic from the underlying OS-level event loop and GPU draw submissions using a **two-tier asynchronous threading model**:
threading.app_thread: The Application Thread (Main Loop): Coordinates user events, performs route navigations, builds layout trees, and runs CSS specificity cascades. It does not block waiting for GPU fences.
threading.render_thread: The Render Pipeline Thread: Receives serialized widget draw lists (rectangles, borders, box-shadows, and text glyph vertices). It structures these arrays inside double-buffered Vulkan instance pages and submits the buffers to the GPU asynchronously.
threading.p2: This separation ensures that heavy style re-calculations or network routes never cause stuttering or frame drops. The GPU maintains a solid, vertical-synced 60+ FPS presentation loop under heavy loads.

pipeline.title: ⚡ 2. Vulkan Core: Instanced Draw-Call Batching
pipeline.p1: Rather than issuing individual draw commands to the graphics API for every button, header, and canvas node, the FluxUI Vulkan backend pools resources inside a unified shader pipeline.
pipeline.p2: rounded-rectangle properties (dimensions, rounded corners, border sizes, colors, and shadow blurs) are written to a single **instance page buffer**. A specialized shader parses these bounds and draws the entire interface layout in a **single instanced draw call**.
pipeline.p3: Text strings are batched using a high-fidelity **Font Atlas Descriptor Set**. Glyphs are mapped into a texture atlas, and their corresponding vertex grids are compiled and pushed to a shared vertex queue, triggering draw flushes only when changing scissor dimensions or texture pages.

quick.title: Setup & Compilation Toolchain
quick.p1: FluxUI is engineered to compile out of the box using modern CMake configurations, with no external package managers needed other than a local compiler and a Vulkan runtime loader.
quick.callout_desc: At the root of your workspace, we provide `build_bindings.ps1`, which automates calling CMake configurations, compiles C++ libraries, generates the Rust static `.rlib` bindings, and prepares the Zig module structure.
quick.pre_comment: # Run compilation of C++ Core, Rust FFI, and Zig targets

preprocessor.title: ⚙️ 2. Advanced Preprocessor Compilation Flags
preprocessor.p1: You can tune specific GPU capabilities using CMake cache entries:

table.msaa: Enables Multi-Sample Anti-Aliasing (4x) for sharp vector edges on modern GPUs. Set to `OFF` to maximize performance on low-power devices.
table.vsync: Locks screen frame pacing to vertical intervals. Set to `OFF` to benchmark GPU frame rates exceeding 500+ FPS.
table.font: Bakes Latin and Latin-Extended glyph grids to textures on start. Lowers VRAM requirements and accelerates dynamic font caching.
table.backend: Sets the starting backend preference if selection is set to `Auto`. Options: `VULKAN`, `D3D12`, `METAL`.

handles.title: FFI C-ABI Specification
handles.p1: To guarantee binary compatibility across C++, Rust, and Zig, FluxUI provides a strict, opaque C-ABI boundary.
handles.p2: All API interfaces avoid passing complex C++ structures by value. Instead, they expose opaque pointers that are marshaled safely across language boundaries.
handles.app_handle: `FluxUIApp`: Tracks runtime engine loops, active OS windower queues, and swapchain render thread routines.
handles.widget_handle: `FluxUIWidget`: Represents an addressable node inside the retained tree (button, panel, label). These handles are stored inside Parent Arenas.

event_bus.title: 🔄 1. Event Registration & Router Callbacks
event_bus.p1: FluxUI FFI maps modern event listeners using standardized function pointers. When an input, resize, or routing action occurs, the engine triggers FFI registered hooks:

rules.title: ⚠️ 2. ABI Memory & Ownership Rules
rules.p1: When compiling wrapper layers for Rust and Zig, you must strictly follow these memory rules to avoid undefined pointer behaviors:
rules.step_a_p: Widgets are strictly owned by their parent panels in the layout tree. When a parent container is cleared or deleted, all child `FluxUIWidget` pointers immediately become invalid. Rust and Zig wrappers use runtime references or raw handles carefully without double-freeing.
rules.step_b_p: Any string passed into FFI calls (such as class names or label parameters) is copied immediately inside the engine's dynamic buffers. You can safely discard FFI string pointers once the call returns.
rules.step_c_p: Getter strings return raw pointers to the underlying `std::string` buffers in C++. They remain valid ONLY until the widget's value undergoes changes or the widget is destroyed.

cascade.title: Blink-Style CSS & Cascade
cascade.p1: FluxUI features a sophisticated, modern CSS engine modeled closely after the Chromium/Blink specification.
cascade.rules_p: Matching styling rules are resolved based on specificity priority weightings. Rules are sorted and resolved in the following priority order:

combinators.title: 🌲 Sibling & Ancestral Walks
combinators.p1: FluxUI style rules participate in descendant matching. The engine parses CSS selector lists and walks parent chains from right-to-left:
combinators.descendant: Descendant Selector (`.sidebar .nav-text`): The engine matches `.nav-text`, then walks up the parent layout tree verifying if any ancestor contains class `.sidebar`.
combinators.child: Child Combinator (`.top-bar > .title-group`): Matches if the immediate parent of `.title-group` has the class `.top-bar`.

live_sandbox.title: 🎨 Interactive Flexbox / Layout Tree Simulator
live_sandbox.p1: Tweak the layout properties below to see how FluxUI's Flexbox engine places nodes dynamically!

pseudos.title: 🎯 Selector Pseudo-Classes
pseudos.p1: FluxUI implements standard pseudo-selectors inside the CSS cascading checker, providing strong layout control:
pseudos.is: `:is(.primary, .danger)`: Matches when any layout rule in the parameter list is true.
pseudos.not: `:not(.active)`: Rejects rules when the widget matches the parameter class.
pseudos.where: `:where(.panel)`: Operates identically to `:is()`, but adds **zero** weight to CSS specificity.

media.title: 📱 Viewport @media Queries
media.p1: Stylesheets can register fully responsive conditions. Viewport changes automatically clear layout caches, forcing a style invalidation cycle:

api_ref.title: API & Widget Reference
api_ref.p1: Every widget in FluxUI is created inside Parent Allocator Arenas and is accessed through raw pointers in C++ or safe struct wrappers in Rust and Zig.

app.title: 1. Application Class
app.p1: Manages standard event dispatches, window states, and GPU threads.

panel.title: 2. Panel Class
panel.p1: A modular container node, serving as the equivalent of a `<div>` element in HTML. Used for layout containers, cards, and custom canvases.

input.title: 5. Class TextInput
input.p1: Handles active text input buffers and keyboard listeners.

tutorials.title: Multi-Language Guide
tutorials.p1: Because FluxUI wraps a standard C++ core inside a dynamic FFI layer, you write the exact same structural layouts whether coding in Rust, C++, or Zig.

rust.title: 🦀 Rust Native Bindings
rust.p1: The Rust FFI binder uses safe lifetimes, matches options, and automatically panics/returns errors when initializations lack graphics support.

cpp.title: 🛡️ C++ Native API
cpp.p1: Includes direct access to standard template lists, custom C++ types, and functional lambdas. Layout creation is direct and fast.

zig.title: ⚡ Zig bindings
zig.p1: Directly interacts using Zig's `@cImport` wrapper. Leverages general-purpose allocators and maps standard error unions.

performance.title: Performance Work Landed
performance.p1: FluxUI undergoes rigorous layout profiling to ensure frame execution stays fast and consistent on modern GPU setups.

allocators.title: 1. O(1) Arena Allocation pools
allocators.p1: To prevent memory fragmentation and repeated system-level allocations when modifying layout hierarchies dynamically, FluxUI utilizes **Arena-backed pools**.
allocators.p2: All child nodes added via add() or alias builders are laid out inside an arena memory segment dedicated to their parent node container. When changing routing paths, parent scopes discard the entire memory block instantly.

caching.title: 2. Style Invalidation & Dirty Layout Flags
caching.p1: To save clock cycles during heavy styling resolution:
caching.dirty: Dirty subtree caching: Stable components skip layout recalculates.
caching.align: Left-Aligned Optimization: Common string widgets bypass glyph-width Walks.

font.title: 3. Font Cache Warming
font.p1: To eliminate stuttering when presenting new text sizes:
font.comment2: // Safely release memory occupied by source font bytes

roadmap.title: Roadmap & Future backends
roadmap.p1: FluxUI is committed to native performance. We are actively refining fallback trees to bring other graphic backends to full parity with our Vulkan pipeline.

backends.title: Staged Backend Fallbacks
backends.p1: The default renderer auto-selection implements a clear chain:
backends.vulkan_p: Our main dynamic GPU drawing target featuring full instanced rounded-rectangle structures, mapped dynamic resource pages, and atlas fonts.
backends.dx_p: Exposed in the API. Parity features are currently in staging under compiler flags.
backends.compat_p: Ensures that even if graphics cards fail to load Vulkan drivers, the UI interface falls back cleanly to safe software rendering.

milestones.title: 🗺️ Future Feature Milestones
milestones.keyframes: CSS Keyframe Animations: GPU-interpolated transitions and transforms.
milestones.wasm: WebAssembly Target: Porting core layouts to WASM + WebGL2.
milestones.hot_reload: Runtime Hot-Reloading: Stylesheets monitor filesystem modifications and dynamically update window layouts.
